2603.23485.title: Failure of contextual invariance in gender inference with large language models
2603.23485.paper_summary: Standard evaluation practices assume that large language model (LLM) outputs are stable under contextually equivalent formulations of a task. Here, we test this assumption in the setting of gender inference. Using a controlled pronoun selection task, we introduce minimal, theoretically uninformative discourse context and find that this induces large, systematic shifts in model outputs. Correlations with cultural gender stereotypes, present in decontextualized settings, weaken or disappear once context is introduced, while theoretically irrelevant features, such as the gender of a pronoun for an unrelated referent, become the most informative predictors of model behaviour. A Contextuality-by-Default analysis reveals that, in 19--52\% of cases across models, this dependence persists after accounting for all marginal effects of context on individual outputs and cannot be attributed to simple pronoun repetition. These findings show that LLM outputs violate contextual invariance even under near-identical syntactic formulations, with implications for bias benchmarking and deployment in high-stakes settings.
2603.23485.paper_summary_zh: 標準評估實踐假設大型語言模型（LLM）的輸出在任務的上下文等價表述下是穩定的。在這裡，我們在性別推斷的設定中測試這一假設。通過使用一個受控的代名詞選擇任務，我們引入最小的、理論上無信息的話語上下文，並發現這會引起模型輸出的大規模系統性變化。在去上下文化的環境中，與文化性別刻板印象的相關性在引入上下文後會減弱或消失，而與無關參照的代名詞性別等理論上無關的特徵則成為模型行為中最具信息量的預測因子。上下文性-默認分析顯示，在19--52\%的情況下，這種依賴在考慮所有上下文對個別輸出的邊際效應後仍然存在，並且不能歸因於簡單的代名詞重複。這些發現表明，即使在近乎相同的句法表述下，LLM的輸出也違反了上下文不變性，這對於偏見基準測試和在高風險環境中的應用具有重要意義。
2603.23485.author: Sagar Kumar et.al.
2603.23485.authors: Sagar Kumar, Ariel Flint, Luca Maria Aiello, Andrea Baronchelli
